nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving tributaries, shaded creeks, and forest streams with soft sandy or silty substrate, leaf litter, and overhanging riparian vegetation; typically blackwater or clearwater conditions

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect delicate barbels
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dark substrate to enhance natural coloration

diet.notes: The Fairy Cory is a foraging omnivore that accepts a wide range of foods. High-quality sinking pellets, catfish wafers, granules, and crushed flake foods form a solid staple diet. Regular offerings of live or frozen foods — bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, Tubifex, and mosquito larvae — are beneficial. Blanched vegetables such as cucumber and zucchini are also accepted. Because this species forages along the bottom, it should not rely on leftovers from other tank inhabitants; target feeding with sinking foods ensures it receives adequate nutrition.

avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: MaurizioCorydoras; 4032x3024. Sharp, well-lit whole fish foraging on pale sand; clearly shows the diagnostic dark eye mask and fine dark body spotting on a silvery body. Lone specimen (no CC group shot available).
karsten_s; 600x300. Clean full lateral profile showing the dark eye mask and mosaic spotting; different angle from the recommended pick. Low resolution, kept as a secondary extra.

summary: The Fairy Cory (Corydoras atropersonatus) is a small, peaceful catfish from Ecuador and Peru, recognised by its dark facial mask and fine body patterning. It reaches about 5 cm and does best in groups of at least six in soft, slightly acidic water. A 2024 revision placed it under the name Hoplisoma atropersonatum.
careNotes: The Fairy Cory is a small, peaceful bottom-dweller from the Tigre and Nanay drainage systems of Ecuador and Peru, inhabiting soft, slightly acidic, slow-moving forest streams. It reaches approximately 4.5–5 cm and is among the more delicate-looking Corydoras, adorned with a dark mask across the eyes and fine mosaic patterning. A group of at least 6 individuals is strongly recommended — solitary or pairs show stress and reduced activity. Soft, fine sand substrate is essential; coarse gravel abrades their sensitive barbels, which can lead to bacterial infection and rapid deterioration. A minimum 60 L tank with good filtration and regular water changes maintains the soft, slightly acidic conditions preferred. Like all Corydoras, the Fairy Cory is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dart to the surface to gulp air — a small gap between water surface and cover glass is important. Low-moderate lighting with ample cover of driftwood, leaf litter, and plants provides security. Avoid hard alkaline water; this species does best below pH 7.0 and in very soft water (gH 1–12). Compatible with small, peaceful community species of similar size. A 2024 phylogenomic revision resurrected the genus Hoplisoma, placing this species under the name Hoplisoma atropersonatum — a reclassification that is pending broader adoption across the aquarium trade and species databases. Warning: like all Corydoras/Hoplisoma, the pectoral and dorsal fin spines can inflict a painful wound and are venomous — handle with care when netting.
breedingNotes: Moderately easy to breed in soft, slightly acidic water. Use a ratio of 2 males per female. Condition breeders with frequent live and frozen foods. Trigger spawning with a large (50–70%) water change using cooler, soft water and increased oxygenation; repeat daily until spawning occurs. During the T-position embrace, females hold 2–4 eggs between their pelvic fins at a time, scattering up to 75 adhesive eggs total across plant leaves and tank surfaces. Remove adults after spawning. Incubation is approximately 3–4 days at 22–24°C. Once fry have absorbed their yolk sacs, offer microworm, Artemia nauplii, and fine powdered fry foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras atropersonatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (45–50 mm SL), temperature (21–27°C), pH (5.0–7.0), hardness (36–179 ppm ≈ gH 2–10), native range (Tigre and Nanay drainages, Ecuador/Peru), habitat, diet (foraging omnivore), breeding protocol (2:1 male/female ratio, cool-water trigger, 3–4 day incubation), and behavioral notes (peaceful, gregarious, groups of 4–6+).

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras atropersonatus (Hoplisoma atropersonatum)
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 4.5 cm SL, temperature 21–24°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness dH 2–25, family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes, South America / Upper Amazon basin distribution, demersal freshwater. Notes genus as Hoplisoma atropersonatum following the 2024 Dias et al. revision.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoradinae revision changes names for hundreds of species
additional.notes: Covered the 2024 Dias et al. phylogenomic revision (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) resurrecting Hoplisoma for former Corydoras lineage L6/L9 species, placing atropersonatus as Hoplisoma atropersonatum. Confirms broad trade adoption is pending.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Fairy Cory (Hoplisoma atropersonatum)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (4.5 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (up to 18 dH), substrate requirements (sandy), group size (5+), diet (pellets, granules, flake, frozen foods), and breeding T-position details (up to 75 eggs scattered). Lists species under new name Hoplisoma atropersonatum.

additional.name: Aquarium Fish Source — Corydoras atropersonatus Fairy Spotted Cory Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~4.5 cm), temperature (21–24°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dGH), tank minimum (75 L / 20 gal), diet (omnivore — pellets, frozen foods, vegetables), native range (Ecuador and Peru, Upper Amazon). Corroborates barbel sensitivity to coarse substrate.

fish.breedingNotes: The Fairy Cory is an egg-scatterer that spawns in the classic Corydoras T-position embrace. A ratio of two males per female is generally recommended. Spawning is typically triggered by large, cool water changes. Up to 75 adhesive eggs are scattered across plant leaves and tank surfaces during the spawning event. Adults should be removed after spawning is complete. Fry accept microworm and Artemia nauplii once they have absorbed their yolk sacs.
